questions.question_text: Missouri follows which system for dividing property in a divorce?
distractors: Community property with equal 50/50 split
Common-law property based solely on title
Tenancy by the entirety with automatic division
questions.explanation: Missouri is an equitable distribution state, NOT a community property state. Courts divide marital property in a way that is fair (equitable) but not necessarily equal. Marital property includes assets acquired during marriage regardless of whose name is on the title.

questions.question_text: A Missouri couple divorces. The husband purchased a rental property during the marriage using only his income. The property is titled in his name only. How is it classified?
questions.correct_answer: Marital property subject to equitable division
distractors: Separate property belonging solely to the husband
Community property split equally between spouses
Joint tenancy property requiring partition
questions.explanation: In Missouri, property acquired during marriage is classified as marital property regardless of whose name appears on the title or whose income was used. It is subject to equitable (fair, not necessarily equal) distribution in divorce.

questions.question_text: A Missouri resident inherited a cabin from her grandmother before marriage. After 15 years of marriage, is this cabin marital property?
questions.correct_answer: No, inherited property is separate property
distractors: Yes, after 10 years it becomes marital property
Yes, all real estate becomes marital after marriage
Only if the spouse contributed to its upkeep
questions.explanation: In Missouri, property acquired before marriage, by gift, or by inheritance is classified as separate (nonmarital) property. However, if the owner commingles it with marital assets (e.g., using marital funds for improvements), it risks partial reclassification.

questions.correct_answer: No, but recognizes valid ones from other states
distractors: Yes, after 7 years of cohabitation
Yes, if the couple files a declaration
No, and it does not recognize any from other states
questions.explanation: Missouri does NOT recognize common-law marriage created within the state. However, Missouri does recognize valid common-law marriages from other states where such marriages are legally established.

questions.question_text: In Missouri's equitable distribution system, 'equitable' means property division will be:
questions.correct_answer: Fair, but not necessarily a 50/50 split
distractors: An exact 50/50 division in all cases
Based solely on each spouse's income
Determined by which spouse holds the title
questions.explanation: Equitable distribution means the court divides marital property fairly based on factors like each spouse's contributions, economic circumstances, and conduct. Fair does not mean equal; a 60/40 or 70/30 split is possible depending on circumstances.

questions.question_text: Missouri assesses residential real property at what percentage of market value?
distractors: 32% of market value
12% of market value
33.33% of market value
questions.explanation: Missouri uses a classified assessment system. Residential property is assessed at 19% of market value. Commercial property is assessed at 32%, agricultural at 12%, and personal property at 33.33%.

questions.question_text: A Missouri commercial building has a market value of $500,000. What is its assessed value?
distractors: $95,000 at the 19% residential rate
$60,000 at the 12% agricultural rate
$166,650 at the 33.33% personal property rate
questions.explanation: Missouri commercial property is assessed at 32% of market value. $500,000 x 0.32 = $160,000 assessed value. This is significantly higher than the 19% residential rate, reflecting Missouri's classified assessment system.

questions.question_text: Missouri reassesses real property on what schedule?
distractors: Annually on January 1 of each year
Every 4 years during election years
Every 3 years on a rolling county basis
questions.explanation: Missouri reassesses real property every 2 years on ODD-numbered years (2023, 2025, 2027, etc.), with values determined as of January 1 of the odd year and carried into the even year (RSMo 137.115).

questions.question_text: A Missouri home's assessed value increased 18% during reassessment. What limitation applies?
questions.correct_answer: Over 15% requires a physical on-site inspection
distractors: All increases are capped at 10% per reassessment cycle
No limitation exists on assessment increases in Missouri
The homeowner must file a formal appeal within 30 days
questions.explanation: Under RSMo 137.115.10-.11, before increasing a residential parcel's assessed valuation more than 15% since the last assessment (excluding new construction and improvements), the assessor must conduct a physical on-site inspection -- personal observation of the exterior, with an interior review only if the owner requests one -- and must notify the owner in writing of the inspection and the owner's rights.

questions.question_text: In its ratio studies, the Missouri State Tax Commission requires assessors' appraised values to fall within what range of actual market value?
distractors: 80% to 120% of market value
95% to 105% of market value
85% to 115% of market value
questions.explanation: Through its ratio studies, the Missouri State Tax Commission requires the market values set by county assessors to fall within 90% to 110% of actual market value (the IAAO standard) before the statutory classification percentages are applied. This ensures uniformity across counties.

questions.question_text: The Hancock Amendment to Missouri's constitution primarily limits what?
questions.correct_answer: Tax rate increases when property values rise
distractors: The total number of taxing districts per county
Individual property assessment amounts
State sales tax on real estate transactions
questions.explanation: The 1980 Hancock Amendment requires that tax RATES be rolled back if property values rise faster than inflation. It limits levy growth to the lesser of actual growth, inflation, or 5%. It does NOT cap individual property assessments.

questions.question_text: Property values in a Missouri county rose 8% while inflation was 3%. Under the Hancock Amendment, the tax levy must be:
questions.correct_answer: Rolled back so revenue growth matches inflation
distractors: Kept the same with no adjustment needed
Increased by 8% to match property values
Reduced by 5% below the current rate
questions.explanation: The Hancock Amendment requires tax rates to be rolled back when values rise faster than inflation. The levy growth is limited to the lesser of actual growth (8%), inflation (3%), or 5%. In this case, the 3% inflation rate controls, so revenue growth is limited to 3%.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes homestead-based property tax relief in Missouri?
questions.correct_answer: No general homestead exemption; relief comes through tax credits
distractors: A $25,000 homestead exemption automatically applies to all primary residences
Assessed value on every owner-occupied home is frozen statewide
Homestead property tax relief is limited to first-time homebuyers
questions.explanation: Missouri has no general homestead exemption that reduces assessed value for all homeowners. Relief is credit-based: the Circuit Breaker credit for qualifying low-income seniors and disabled residents (including renters), the county-adopted senior tax freeze credit for owners age 62 and older (RSMo 137.1050), and the SB 3 (2025) homestead credit (RSMo 137.1055), put to county votes in April 2026, which freezes or caps growth in a homestead's tax liability with no age requirement. (A narrow constitutional exemption exists for former prisoners of war with a total service-connected disability, and the separate $15,000 homestead exemption of RSMo 513.475 protects against judgment creditors, not property taxes.)

questions.question_text: Missouri's Circuit Breaker tax credit program benefits which group of residents?
distractors: All homeowners regardless of age or income
First-time homebuyers under age 35
Veterans with service-connected disabilities only
questions.explanation: Missouri's Circuit Breaker (Property Tax Credit, RSMo 135.010-135.030) aids low-income residents who are 65 or older, disabled, 100% service-connected disabled veterans, or age 60+ receiving surviving-spouse Social Security benefits -- including qualifying renters. HB 594 (2025) expanded the credit effective 2026, raising the maximum to $1,550 for owners and $1,055 for renters, with inflation indexing from 2027.

questions.question_text: HB 594, effective August 28, 2025, made Missouri the first state with an income tax to do what?
questions.correct_answer: Eliminate individual capital gains tax entirely
distractors: Eliminate property taxes for seniors over 70
Remove all transfer taxes on real estate sales
Cap annual property tax increases at 2%
questions.explanation: HB 594 made Missouri the first state that levies an income tax to fully exempt individual capital gains, via a 100% deduction under RSMo 143.121 applying to tax years beginning on or after January 1, 2025. This has significant implications for real estate investors selling properties in Missouri. (A corporate exemption triggers only if the top individual rate falls to 4.5% or below.)
